narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 62.6% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records and outcomes. This threshold is set to prioritize admitting those most likely to succeed on the bar, ensuring the program's reputation and resources go to students with the strongest projected paths forward. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the middle-upper range typical for those who passed the bar—pushing the prediction heavily toward expecting a failure. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level compared to the much higher averages for bar passers, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential factor, signaling early academic struggles that often persist. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 39 stood out as a strong positive force, well above the average for successful students, and it nearly offset some of those worries by suggesting solid test-taking ability. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, while not the top driver here, sat noticeably below the 3.24 average of bar passers, adding to the overall risk profile. Coming from an upper-middle income family and planning full-time enrollment aligns well with many who succeed—over 90% of bar passers were full-time students, and your bracket is common among them—but it couldn't outweigh the academic decile red flags. In essence, the model saw your low class rankings in key years as the biggest hurdles, unlike accepted applicants who typically rank in the top half or better early on. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background; it's a data-driven call to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, focus on boosting your grades to climb those deciles—perhaps through tutoring, study groups, or lighter course loads—and retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength. Consider reapplying after a post-bac program or targeted prep to demonstrate growth, as many students turn things around with clear improvement plans. You're already showing promise in areas like your LSAT; building on that could shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you have the drive to redefine your story.
